text: Jacob heard Laban's sons complaining he had taken all their father's wealth and made his fortune from it.
Jacob noticed Laban's attitude toward him was no longer friendly.
The LORD told Jacob to return to his homeland, promising to be with him.
Jacob sent for Rachel and Leah to meet him out in the field where his flocks were.
He told them Laban's attitude had changed, but the God of his father had been with him.
He reminded them he had served their father with all his strength.
Yet Laban had cheated him and changed his wages ten times, though God did not let him harm Jacob.
Whenever Laban said the spotted ones would be Jacob's wages, all the flocks bore spotted young, and so on.
So God had taken away Laban's livestock and given it to Jacob.
Jacob described a dream where the male goats breeding the flock were streaked, spotted, and speckled.
The angel of God called to him in the dream, and he answered.
The angel told him to look, for all the male goats were streaked and spotted, since God had seen Laban's mistreatment of him.
The angel identified himself as the God of Bethel, where Jacob anointed the pillar and made a vow, telling him now to leave this land and return to his native land.
Rachel and Leah answered that they had no share left in their father's estate.
They felt treated as foreigners, sold, and had their money used up by their father.
All the wealth God took from their father belonged to them and their children, so Jacob should do as God said.
Jacob set his children and wives on camels.
He drove all his livestock and possessions gathered in Paddan-aram and set out for Isaac his father in Canaan.
While Laban was away shearing his sheep, Rachel stole her father's household gods.
Jacob deceived Laban the Aramean by not telling him he was fleeing.
He fled with everything he had, crossed the Euphrates, and headed toward the hill country of Gilead.
On the third day Laban was told that Jacob had fled.
Laban took his relatives and pursued Jacob seven days, catching him in the hill country of Gilead.
God came to Laban in a dream at night, warning him to say nothing to Jacob, good or bad.
Laban overtook Jacob, who had pitched his tent in the hill country; Laban camped there too.
Laban accused Jacob of deceiving him and carrying off his daughters like captives of war.
He asked why Jacob fled secretly instead of allowing a proper farewell with music.
Laban complained he wasn't allowed to kiss his grandchildren and daughters goodbye, calling it foolish.
Laban said he had the power to harm Jacob, but God had warned him the night before to say nothing.
Laban understood Jacob longed for home, but asked why he stole his gods.
Jacob said he was afraid Laban would take his daughters from him by force.
Jacob said whoever had the gods would not live; he did not know Rachel had taken them, so Laban searched everything.
Laban searched Jacob's tent, Leah's tent, and the two maids' tents, but found nothing; he went into Rachel's tent.
Rachel had hidden the idols inside her camel's saddle and was sitting on them; Laban searched but did not find them.
She told her father not to be angry that she could not rise before him, since she was in her time of the month; so he did not find the idols.
Jacob became angry and confronted Laban, asking what crime he had committed to be hunted down.
He challenged Laban to show what he had found among his possessions and set it before their relatives to judge.
Jacob said he had served twenty years, and Laban's sheep and goats never miscarried, nor did he eat the rams.
He did not bring Laban torn animals but bore the loss himself, whether stolen by day or night.
He endured heat by day and frost by night, with sleep fleeing from his eyes.
Twenty years he served Laban, fourteen for the daughters and six for the flocks, while Laban changed his wages ten times.
If not for the God of his fathers, Laban would have sent him away empty; but God had seen his affliction and rebuked Laban last night.
Laban answered that the daughters, children, and flocks were all his, but he could do nothing about it now.
So Laban proposed they make a covenant as a witness between them.
Jacob took a stone and set it up as a pillar.
He told his relatives to gather stones, and they made a heap and ate there by it.
Laban called it Jegar-sahadutha, and Jacob called it Galeed.
Laban said the heap was a witness between them that day, so it was named Galeed.
It was also called Mizpah, for Laban said may the LORD watch between them when they are apart.
Laban warned Jacob not to mistreat his daughters or take other wives, since God was witness between them.
Laban said, here is this heap and pillar I have set between us.
This heap and pillar were a witness that neither would cross over to harm the other.
May the God of Abraham and Nahor judge between them; Jacob swore by the fear of his father Isaac.
Jacob offered a sacrifice on the mountain and invited his relatives to eat bread; they ate and spent the night on the mountain.
Early the next morning Laban kissed his grandchildren and daughters, blessed them, and returned home.
